title: HW2 Q5 — Sort a 'Close-to-Sorted' Array in $O(n)$
statement: $A$ of size $n$ differs from its sorted version in at most $\log_2 n$ positions. Sort $A$ in $O(n)$.

steps.title: Step 1 — scan and push to a monotone stack.
Step 2 — $S$ misses at most $2\log_2 n$ elements.
steps.body:
**Claim:** every time we 'leave out' a pair of elements (in the else branch), at least one of them was out of place in the original array.

**Proof.** If we pop $A[j]$ when processing $A[i]$ with $A[i] < S.\text{top}() = A[j]$ and $j < i$: if both were in place in the sorted array, since $j < i$ we'd have $A[j] \le A[i]$. Contradicts $A[i] < A[j]$. So at least one is out of place.

Since at most $\log_2 n$ elements are out of place, we leave out at most $2 \log_2 n$ elements. So $|T| \le 2 \log_2 n$.

steps.title: Step 3 — $S$ is a sorted subsequence.
steps.body: By construction, $S$ contains elements in pushed-order which was non-decreasing (each push requires $A[i] \ge S.\text{top}()$). So $S$ is sorted. ✓

steps.title: Step 4 — sort $T$ and merge.
steps.body:
$|T| \le 2 \log_2 n$. Sort $T$ (e.g. merge sort) in $O(|T| \log |T|) = O(\log n \cdot \log \log n) = o(n)$.

Then merge $S$ and $T$ (both sorted) in $O(n)$.

**Total runtime: $O(n)$.**

tips.label: Why $\log n \cdot \log \log n = o(n)$
tips.content: Both factors are logarithms — polynomials beat any product of logarithms.

answerKey: Scan and maintain monotone stack $S$ (contains most of $A$, sorted). Spill-over list $T$ has $\le 2\log_2 n$ elements. Sort $T$ (cheap) and merge. Total $O(n)$.
